title: Summer at Your Side
description: Adachi and Shimamura walk along the beach at sunset during their last summer vacation of high school. A quiet, tender moment of connection.
content:
The evening breeze brushed my cheek, carrying grains of sand that made it feel like the wind itself was touching me. The beach glowed in the sunset, and maybe it was just the mood, but even the sand felt lighter underfoot—nudging us toward home.

Far ahead, the setting sun shimmered like a tiny lighthouse breaking across the waves. Summer sunsets were always beautiful, always fleeting.

But someone wasn't looking at the ocean at all. Her eyes were fixed entirely on me.

Look, Adachi—the ocean's gorgeous.

Mm.

Our hands swung between us as we walked, still in our swimsuits, reluctant to leave the sand. The hibiscus flowers tucked behind our ears looked calm now that dusk was settling in. It was our last summer vacation of high school. We'd spent the whole day playing at the beach, and now it was finally time to head back.

I was happy—really, deeply happy. It had been a long time since joy felt this real. Before coming here I worried the beach might be boring, but the day had flown by. Time that felt empty when I was alone became perfectly full with her at my side. We hadn't even done everything yet, and already I was excited for next time.

I was glad we came. Every step on the sand stirred warm feelings in my chest. There was so much I wanted to remember. The sunset washing over the beach was one of them.

But Adachi didn't seem interested in scenery at all.

It's pretty over there too, I said, but she didn't look. Her eyes stayed on me.

If she wanted to stare at me, she could do that anytime—even after we went home. Maybe thinking that way meant I still didn't quite 'understand' her.

Oh wow, so picturesque, I said dramatically, pretending to admire the ocean, then quickly turned toward her. With perfect timing—our eyes met. Adachi froze like a startled animal, panic spinning in her gaze. It was exactly the reaction I wanted, and it satisfied me instantly.

Hey, Adachi. Look—it's me.

Mm…? Uh… yes. I've been looking at you the whole time…

She was confused, but her gaze still didn't wander. 'I've been watching,' she mumbled, scratching her cheek shyly.

Well, you have been staring.

When I pointed it out, Adachi shook her head awkwardly, trying to deny it.

Or maybe, I teased, you've only got eyes for me?

N-not that much…

Her tongue tangled itself as she tried to explain. Knowing she couldn't escape, she finally lifted her head—her posture straightening a little.

Because… the Shimamura I'm looking at right now… exists only at this moment.

If she had to explain why she kept staring, that was her answer. Her eyes darted nervously, but her voice was firm.

…That's a pretty profound way to put it, I said.

So she felt the same as I did—wanting to store this moment in her memory like a beautiful landscape.

Which meant that to Adachi… I was something worth treasuring. Something beautiful.

It was too much praise. And yet… having someone value me like that—…felt nice. Yeah. It felt really nice.

So—'Hey, Adachi. Look—it's me.'

Remembering our linked hands, I faced her again. I wanted her to see me as much as she wanted to look. Adachi squeezed my hand tighter. She froze, staring straight at me. Her gaze flicked down, just for a second—to my chest—and I didn't miss it.

I thought about calling her out…and instead a smile crept onto my lips. Not yet. I'd wait until she really couldn't deny it before saying anything. Not that I'd be totally unharmed when I did. But at least the sunset would hide the color in my face.
